prompt: Conduct a research report on the manufacturing technology options for hollow motor shafts used in New Energy Vehicle (NEV) electric drive units. List all current forming techniques, compare them based on criteria such as suitable materials, cost-effectiveness, required subsequent processing steps, and other relevant factors. Finally, identify the most suitable manufacturing routes for this specific application.
article:
## 1) Application context: what makes NEV EDU hollow motor shafts different

Even though “hollow shaft” is a generic part category, NEV **electric drive unit (EDU) motor/rotor shafts** are typically pushed by a combination of:

- **Very high speed** (industrial evidence for rotor shafts reaching **~20,000 rpm** in e-motors) [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318)  
- **High transmitted torque** (explicitly described as “many times higher than … camshafts” for rotor shafts) [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318)  
- **Tight rotordynamic/NVH constraints**, because EV drivetrains lack the masking noise of an ICE and become more sensitive to excitation from manufacturing deviations and eccentricities (e.g., eccentricity can modulate electromagnetic and gear-mesh forces) [[31]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10010-025-00816-z)  
- **Hollow functions** beyond lightweighting, notably: cavity used for **cooling** in powerful e-motors [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318) and process routes that integrate **internal cooling fins / internal cooling tube** [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/).

These translate into manufacturing constraints that are unusually strict simultaneously on **geometry (runout/concentricity), mass symmetry (balance), surface integrity (fatigue), and joining/fits**.

### 1.1 Key acceptance frameworks that drive manufacturability

**Balancing & residual unbalance**
- Balance quality for rigid rotors is defined by ISO 1940-1 [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html) (superseded in the ISO 21940 series; ISO 21940-11 is widely cited as the successor) [[2]](https://vibromera.eu/glossary/balance-quality/)[[3]](https://vibromera.eu/glossary/g-grade/). Practically, this forces the production route to provide:
  - stable mass distribution (minimize eccentricity from geometry + density variations),
  - accessible correction planes and repeatable correction methods (drill/mill/add weight),
  - and controlled assembly conventions (see next bullet).

**Key convention for shafts with keyed fitments**
- ISO 21940-32 defines the **shaft and fitment key convention** and specifies **half-key** use when balancing individual components so that the assembled rotor meets balance tolerance; it also mandates marking (“H”) on shaft/fitment faces near the keyway [[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf).  
  *Manufacturing implication:* if the shaft has a keyway/coupling feature, the production balancing method, tooling, and traceability marking must be designed in from the start.

**Factory vibration acceptance of electrical machines**
- IEC 60034-14 specifies acceptance vibration test procedures and limits for many rotating electrical machines (120–15,000 rpm range) and references ISO 21940-32; previews indicate it includes limits for **shaft vibration and maximum run-out** (tables exist in the standard, but numeric limits are not present in the accessible preview text) [[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[11]](https://www.normsplash.com/Samples/IEC/169391251/IEC-60034-14-2018-en-fr.pdf).  
  *Manufacturing implication:* runout and balance are not “nice to have” but can be acceptance-gating.

**Runout definition & verification**
- Runout is a standardized GPS control under ISO 1101 (circular runout / total runout concepts are part of the standard) [[14]](https://www.iso.org/obp/ui/), with common industrial measurement by dial indicator and acceptance as max–min within drawing tolerance [[12]](https://www.schlenker-spannwerkzeuge.de/en/wiki/circular-runout/). Secondary metrology explanations distinguish circular vs total runout in the expected way [[13]](https://www.keyence.com/ss/products/measure-sys/gd-and-t/type/run-out-tolerance.jsp).  
  *Manufacturing implication:* the process chain must manage datum strategy, heat-treat distortion, and finish operations (grinding/hard turning) to hold TIR on bearing seats and rotor-fit diameters.

**Fits for bearings and interference joints**
- Shaft/bore tolerance zones and fits rely on ISO 286 (SKF explicitly bases seat tolerances and fit calculations on ISO 286-2) [[19]](https://www.skf.com/group/products/rolling-bearings/principles-of-rolling-bearing-selection/bearing-selection-process/bearing-interfaces/tolerances-and-resultant-fits). SKF provides a practical framework for computing resultant fits (including ±3σ probable fit ranges) from bearing tolerances and seat tolerances [[19]](https://www.skf.com/group/products/rolling-bearings/principles-of-rolling-bearing-selection/bearing-selection-process/bearing-interfaces/tolerances-and-resultant-fits).  
- For press/shrink interference joints (common for rotor-to-shaft assemblies), DIN 7190 is a standard calculation basis; tooling/software notes emphasize inclusion of temperature, friction coefficient, and centrifugal force, plus criteria to avoid “gaping joint” under radial/bending loads [[22]](https://www.tbksoft.com/en/description/shaft-hub-connections.html)[[23]](https://www.eassistant.eu/fileadmin/dokumente/eassistant/etc/HTMLHandbuch/en/eAssistantHandbch15.html).  
  *Manufacturing implication:* final diameters and surface finish at fit interfaces must be process-capable and stable after heat treatment; assembly must control cleanliness/lubrication and temperature.

**Fatigue sizing & surface effects**
- DIN 743 (summarized in an engineering calculation guide) connects endurance capability to stress concentrations, surface roughness, technological size factors, and residual stress effects; it emphasizes that surface roughness at notches and diameter effects influence allowable fatigue amplitude [[24]](https://www.mesys.ch/doc/DIN743_CalculationBasis.pdf).  
  *Manufacturing implication:* forming routes that preserve beneficial grain flow and introduce compressive residual stresses can materially reduce risk, while machining marks at fillets/splines can dominate fatigue.

### 1.2 What “hollow” changes functionally (manufacturing-driven view)
Hollow shafts are used to:
- **reduce mass** (and inertia) while maintaining stiffness,
- enable **internal functions** (coolant passages, internal cooling tube) [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318)[[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/),
- potentially change torsional stiffness and torsional NVH behavior (torsional vibration is a common NVH problem area in e-drive development) [[30]](https://www.sae.org/papers/structural-vibration-analysis-nvh-trouble-shooting-electric-drive-system-2021-01-0703).

So the best manufacturing route is not just “cheapest tube,” but one that can reliably achieve:
- thin wall without instability,
- concentricity/runout after heat treat,
- rotor interface quality (fit, distortion),
- and internal feature integration where cooling is needed.

---

## 2) Manufacturing technology options (forming-focused) for hollow motor shafts

Below is a consolidated list of **current** (industrialized) and **commercially emerging** forming methods that can make hollow motor shafts or their near-net preforms. Many production chains combine several of these.

### 2.1 Tube-based precursor routes (tube is the hollow “starting truth”)

#### A) Seamless tube making via cross-roll piercing + elongation (Mannesmann-type)
**Mechanism / chain**
- Starts from a solid billet → **piercing** → elongation/rolling (mandrel mill, PQF®, push bench CPE/TPE, Assel mill, pilger, etc.) [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants).
- SMS notes piercing is “crucial” for subsequent tube quality; cross-roll piercing is the leading method and can achieve closer wall thickness tolerance vs alternatives; three-roll piercing can further improve wall thickness quality [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants).

**Defect risks (source-explicit)**
- “Onion rings” at pipe ends if the piercing plug is not centered; a billet centering machine reduces this and improves wall thickness uniformity [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants).

**Materials**
- Broadly steels and alloys used in seamless tube industries; specific EDU alloys are not enumerated in the captured sources, but industrial seamless tube lines support high-grade steels [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants).

**Typical role in EDU shafts**
- Often upstream feedstock for later near-net shaft forming: flow forming, swaging, radial forging, machining + finishing.

**Cost / scalability**
- Very mature, high-capex upstream industry; cost-effective at scale when using standard tube sizes (you buy tube instead of owning a piercing mill).

#### B) Cold tube drawing / sinking (including DOM-type process chains)
**Mechanism**
- Tube is die-drawn on drawbenches (hydraulic piston or chain type) [[50]](https://www.mair-research.com/en/cold-draw-benches-tube/)[[51]](https://www.fenn-torin.com/metal-forming-machines/drawing-equipment). Tube drawing is described as key equipment for producing **high-precision seamless steel pipes under cold conditions** [[49]](https://www.senbomachinery.com/cold-drawing-machine/cold-draw-bench/3-line-copper-stainless-alloy-steel-draw.html). Tube suppliers list extensive cold-drawn tube portfolios [[52]](https://www.mannesmann.com/en/tubes-pipes)[[53]](https://www.mannesmann-precision-tubes.com/en/index.html).

**Defect risks (snippet-level evidence)**
- Common cold tube drawing defects include “non uniform, undesired deformations” [[55]](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-drawing-machine-the-redesigned-drawing-die-and-the-drawn-tube_fig1_232402391). A snippet also indicates some surface concavities may persist through drawing [[54]](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/arious-types-of-surface-defects-observed-in-production-a-longitudinal-b-transverse_fig1_348265418) (limited context).

**Strengths for EDU shafts**
- High dimensional precision of starting tube; good for thin-walled shafts that later receive local forming (swaging/spinning/flow forming).
- Good supply chain availability (precision tube suppliers) [[52]](https://www.mannesmann.com/en/tubes-pipes)[[53]](https://www.mannesmann-precision-tubes.com/en/index.html).

**Likely downstream steps**
- Cut-to-length, straightening, local end forming (swage/spin), machining of seats/splines, heat treat, grinding, balancing.

#### C) Welded tube + cold drawing / size rolling
Tube producers explicitly supply **welded & drawn** and **welded & size-rolled** precision tubes [[52]](https://www.mannesmann.com/en/tubes-pipes)[[53]](https://www.mannesmann-precision-tubes.com/en/index.html).

**Implication**
- Potentially lower-cost tube feedstock than seamless, but weld seam and HAZ integrity become key risks for high-speed rotor shafts; this increases NDT burden (not quantified in sources, but inherent).

---

### 2.2 Near-net hollow shaft forming from tube (dominant for thin-wall + high concentricity)

#### D) Flow forming / spinning (tube flow forming; rotary forming on mandrel)
**Mechanism (explicit)**
- Tube preform pushed onto mandrel; rollers press from outside; material flows axially → **wall thickness reduces and length increases** [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html). Forward/backward variants can produce internally toothed parts [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html).  
- Spinning forms rotationally symmetric hollow bodies by incremental roller forming on a chuck/mandrel; flanging/cutoff can be integrated in one clamping [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html).

**Industrial capability and adoption cues**
- WF Maschinenbau describes **one-piece rotor shafts**: “firstly flow-formed” (with integration of **internal cooling fins** and/or external stop collar), achieving **radial run-out tolerances < 0.05 mm**, then shaft end is drawn in (optionally integrating internal cooling tube) [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/).  
- Grotnes positions tube spin-forming as economical and highlights avoiding welded end-cone transitions (reducing assembly/welding costs) [[67]](https://grotnes.com/spinning/).

**Defect risks**
- Not enumerated in captured sources for flow forming specifically; in practice, risk centers around wall thinning limits, surface pickup, and waviness—but those are not source-supported here, so consider them as engineering expectations rather than cited facts.

**Best-fit use cases**
- High-speed rotor shafts needing excellent runout, thin wall, and integrated cooling features (internal fins/tube) [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/).

**Typical downstream steps**
- Minimal machining on OD possible if runout is as-formed; still typically needs:
  - precision machining/grinding of bearing journals and rotor interfaces,
  - heat treatment (induction/case hardening depending on design),
  - final grinding/superfinishing,
  - dynamic balancing per ISO framework [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)[[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf).

#### E) Rotary swaging (cold incremental radial hammering; with/without mandrel)
**Mechanism (explicit)**
- Multiple die segments oscillate radially and strike the workpiece at >1,000 strokes/min, creating incremental deformation steps ~0.25–1.5 mm [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/). Can be done with internal mandrels to create internal splines/tapers and other internal geometries, sometimes removing machining steps [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/).  
- Machine capability examples: workpiece diameter 0.8–120 mm; pipe length 25–6000 mm [[61]](https://felss.com/en/maschine/rotary-swaging-machinesha/).

**Benefits (explicit claims)**
- Cold working increases strength and fatigue resistance; Felss claims fatigue strength can increase **up to 30%** (vendor claim) [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/).  
- Can produce stepped/tapered/polygonal/spline-like geometries [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/).

**Adoption cues**
- An aerospace user reports achieving **tight runout tolerances** and high surface quality on steel tubes with a new rotary swaging machine (2022 equipment purchase) [[62]](https://www.ro-ra.com/corporate-news/2023/swaging-of-tie-rod-tubes/). (Aerospace ≠ automotive, but it evidences technical capability.)

**Best-fit use cases**
- Tube-based shafts requiring stepped ends, spline-like features, local diameter reductions, or internal features with mandrels.

**Downstream steps**
- Similar to flow forming: heat treat + finish grinding/machining for journals and fit surfaces, plus balancing.

---

### 2.3 Forging/rolling routes for hollow shaft preforms (dominant for thick-wall + fatigue robustness + high volumes)

#### F) Radial forging (with mandrels for hollow contours)
**Mechanism / capability (explicit)**
- Incremental forming with typically 4 radially oscillating tools; used chip-less for highly stressed drivetrain parts and increasingly for e-mobility [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).  
- GFM explicitly states inner diameters are costly to machine but can be produced “quite easily” by radial forging; complex hollow contours can be produced in one operation using forging mandrels; can be cold/semi-hot/hot [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).  
- GFM claims **>10 million shafts per year** are produced using their radial forging technology (broad claim, not limited to EV motor shafts) [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).

**Strengths**
- Good for high-volume production, robust properties, and efficient creation of internal diameters without deep-hole drilling [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).

**Downstream**
- Typically requires finish machining/grinding on critical seats; heat treat; balancing.

#### G) Cross-wedge rolling (CWR) of (hollow) shaft preforms
**Mechanism**
- Cylindrical billets deformed between rolls with wedge dies to form closer-to-final shapes with high productivity and material utilization [[75]](https://www.alekvs.com/cross-wedge-rolling-machine/).

**Evidence of motor-shaft-specific relevance**
- A research publication explicitly addresses “cross wedge rolling process for **hollow motor shaft**,” noting advantages in refined grains and improved mechanical properties [[76]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384053671_Design_and_simulation_of_cross_wedge_rolling_process_for_hollow_motor_shaft).

**Defect risk (explicit)**
- “Central cracking” can form internal cavities in CWR products [[78]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/363857190_Investigation_and_prediction_of_central_cracking_in_cross_wedge_rolling). Another fragment indicates ovality/necking are monitored in CWR defect control studies [[79]](https://www.researchsquare.com/article/rs-3819506/latest.pdf) (fragmentary).

**Best-fit**
- High-volume near-net preforms that later receive finish machining; especially where forging-like grain flow and high throughput are valued.

#### H) Backward/forward extrusion with mandrel (bulk extrusion)
**Industrial presence**
- Press makers present forward and backward extrusion as standard capabilities [[84]](https://www.schulergroup.com/major/us/technologien/produkte/grundlagen_massivumformung_fliesspressen/index.html).  
- One technical note about hollow extrusion highlights friction over a straight mandrel generating heat due to shear as metal flows over the mandrel [[85]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4701/8/6/380)—a real process-design concern for tool life and surface quality.

**Best-fit**
- Near-net hollow creation in compact steps; often paired with subsequent calibration and machining.

#### I) “Cold forging hollow motor shaft” (example productized capability)
- A Japanese supplier product example states a **forged hollow motor shaft for EV** is produced by **cold forging**, with “deep hole, keyway motor shafts,” giving example accuracies: key groove ±0.1 mm, OD ±0.6 mm, hole depth 130 mm, hole diameter 29 mm, and applicable material S45C and other carbon steels [[113]](https://mono.ipros.com/en/product/detail/2000621393/).  
  This is one of the few sources with explicit numeric dimensional capability for a forged hollow motor shaft preform.

---

### 2.4 Other forming options (more situational for EDU shafts)

#### J) Roll forming / rotary roll forming (rings/cylinders)
- Tube/pipe mill roll forming is an established route to make welded tubes (Yoder describes roll forming + tube/pipe mills and cage forming history) [[73]](https://www.formtekgroup.com/yoder/about-yoder/).  
- Rotary roll forming forms profiles in cylinders/rings between male/female dies; can displace material axially/radially for near net shape rings and is used in high-value alloys in aerospace [[74]](https://www.grotnes.com/2024/06/12/discover-the-precision-and-versatility-of-grotnes-rotary-roll-forming-technology/).  
**For EDU shafts:** generally more relevant to making tube feedstock or ring-like features than the full shaft body, but may appear in component ecosystems.

#### K) Tube hydroforming
**Mechanism**
- Uses high-pressure fluid to shape tubes into complex lightweight structures [[86]](https://www.intran.mx/how-tube-hydroforming-is-advancing-automotive-manufacturing/). Schuler describes hydroforming production lines capable with aluminum and AHSS and integrated processes like bending/preforming/hydroforming/laser cutting [[87]](https://hydroforming.schulergroup.com/en/parts/index.html)[[88]](https://hydroforming.schulergroup.com/).

**Defect modes (explicit)**
- Tube hydroforming common failure modes: **buckling, wrinkling, bursting** [[90]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222018107_Hydroforming_of_aluminum_extrusion_tubes_for_automotive_applications_Part_I_Buckling_wrinkling_and_bursting_analyses_of_aluminum_tubes)[[91]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/260814621_Wrinkling_Study_in_Tube_Hydroforming_Process).

**For EDU shafts**
- Hydroforming is more typical for non-rotating structures; for rotating shafts, it’s niche, but could be relevant for complex local geometry or attachments—must be weighed against runout and wall thickness control demands.

#### L) Electromagnetic forming / Magnetic Pulse Forming (MPF) / EMPT
**Mechanism**
- A pulsed magnetic field accelerates a metallic workpiece at high speed into a die; high deformation speed increases formability and reduces springback [[93]](https://www.bmax.com/technology/forming).  
- EMPT vendors claim industrial mass production machine series availability [[92]](https://www.pstproducts.com/en/empt_machine_systems_pstproducts.html).

**Limitations noted**
- Solenoid coil tooling may face industrial issues due to “poor rigidity” (preview from electromagnetic crimping research) [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1526612524001270).

**For EDU shafts**
- More commonly a joining/calibration process (e.g., crimping/sleeving) than the primary method for producing the entire shaft; promising for localized forming without tool contact, but requires specialized power electronics and coil consumables.

#### M) Powder metallurgy (PM) / MIM near-net parts assembled into hollow sections
- PM can make high-precision components cost-effectively at scale by compacting and sintering powders [[96]](https://www.gknpm.com/en/technologies/conventional-powder-metallurgy/).  
- A PM manufacturer notes “hollow sections can be formed by combining, sintering, and brazing parts with high precision” [[95]](https://www.fine-sinter.com/en/products/pmp/technology/panel3/post-11.php).

**For EDU shafts**
- Full PM shafts are uncommon for high-speed rotor shafts due to fatigue and defect sensitivity concerns (not source-stated here); PM is more plausible for integrated features or subcomponents, or for assemblies where brazing/joining forms a hollow.

---

## 3) Downstream processing steps by route (what you *still* must do)

Real-world e-drive rotor shaft manufacturing is typically **process-chain engineering** more than a single operation.

### 3.1 Common finishing steps that dominate final capability

**Heat treatment + distortion management**
- Industrial e-drive component providers explicitly include hardening and grinding in their process spectrum. Hirschvogel cites a broad “spectrum of processes” including extrusion, swaging, hardening, and grinding in **IT3 quality** for a single-part hollow rotor shaft example [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).  
- For a joined hollow rotor shaft, Hirschvogel lists: **forged → machined → laser-welded → induction hardened → hard machined → ground** [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).  
- Production system providers emphasize **low-distortion carburizing** as a key capability for drivetrain components (JTEKT) [[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html).

**Precision machining/grinding of journals and interfaces**
- Bearing seats and rotor-fit diameters typically require finishing to ISO fit classes (SKF fit framework references ISO 286-2) [[19]](https://www.skf.com/group/products/rolling-bearings/principles-of-rolling-bearing-selection/bearing-selection-process/bearing-interfaces/tolerances-and-resultant-fits).  
- Grinding takt-time and consolidation (wide wheel cylindrical grinding) is positioned as a productivity lever for stepped shafts [[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html).

**Balancing**
- Must align with ISO rotor balancing principles [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html) and keyed assembly conventions where applicable [[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf), and with IEC 60034-14 acceptance testing environment [[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[11]](https://www.normsplash.com/Samples/IEC/169391251/IEC-60034-14-2018-en-fr.pdf).

**Joining / assembly for hollow and/or assembled designs**
- Assembled (joined) hollow rotor shafts are explicitly industrialized (thyssenkrupp calls rotor shafts an “assembled design,” leveraging camshaft assembly know-how) [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318).  
- Hirschvogel’s joined shaft chain explicitly uses **laser welding** [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).  
- Laser welding is highlighted as enabling compact, high-strength, reproducible connections in electrified powertrains (including rotor shafts) [[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/).

**Technical cleanliness**
- Hirschvogel explicitly states rotor shafts have high demands including torque, run-out, and **technical cleanliness** and includes “washing and packing according to cleanliness requirements” as part of its process offer [[101]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/en/products/electric-motor/rotor-shaft/).

### 3.2 Inspection / NDT implications (often underappreciated)
- Hollow parts add internal defect modes and inspection challenges. An ultrasonic inspection system for hollow axles/shafts describes detecting transverse/longitudinal/volume defects with local immersion UT, with ~10 min inspection time per axis and 97% availability [[118]](https://www.nds.actemium.de/en/products/ultrasonic-inspection-of-hollow-axles/).  
  While rail axles are not motor shafts, it illustrates the kind of NDT infrastructure hollow rotating parts can require at scale.

---

## 4) Comparative scorecard of forming techniques (NEV hollow motor shaft lens)

**Assumptions (explicit):** Because the captured source set contains limited quantitative cost and tolerance data across all processes, the comparison uses (i) source-explicit numeric claims where available, and (ii) qualitative, manufacturing-standard criteria (CAPEX/OPEX, throughput, defect risk, downstream needs). Where a criterion is not source-supported, it’s framed as an engineering inference, not a cited fact.

### 4.1 Summary table (high-level)

| Technique | Typical starting stock | Best-suited materials (from sources) | Geometry / key strengths (source-based) | Noted defect risks (source-based) | Downstream processing burden | Cost-effectiveness / volume fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Seamless piercing + tube rolling (Mannesmann route) | Billet → hollow shell/tube | High-grade steels supported by seamless tube industry [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants) | Piercing quality critical; cross-roll piercing gives advantages; 3-roll improves wall thickness quality [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants) | “Onion rings” if plug not centered [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants) | Used upstream; needs later forming/machining/HT | Very mature; economical if buying standard seamless tube |
| Cold tube drawing/sinking | Seamless/welded tube | Precision steel tubes widely supplied [[52]](https://www.mannesmann.com/en/tubes-pipes)[[53]](https://www.mannesmann-precision-tubes.com/en/index.html) | High-precision tubes; draw benches and automation options [[50]](https://www.mair-research.com/en/cold-draw-benches-tube/)[[51]](https://www.fenn-torin.com/metal-forming-machines/drawing-equipment) | Non-uniform undesired deformation [[55]](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-drawing-machine-the-redesigned-drawing-die-and-the-drawn-tube_fig1_232402391) | Requires end forming + finish machining/HT | Excellent for mass supply of precursors |
| Flow forming (tube) | Tube on mandrel | Not specified; typically steels/Al (Schuler hydroforming mentions Al/AHSS but that’s different) | Wall thinning + lengthening mechanism [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html); rotor shafts claimed runout <0.05 mm and integration of cooling fins/tube [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/) | Not enumerated in sources | Medium: finish journals + HT + balance | Very strong for high-speed thin-wall shafts and integrated cooling [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/) |
| Spinning / spin-forming | Tube/blank | Not specified | Economical tube forming; reduces need for welded transitions [[67]](https://grotnes.com/spinning/) | Not enumerated in sources | Medium | Good for end forming and transitions, especially automated cells [[67]](https://grotnes.com/spinning/) |
| Rotary swaging | Tube or solid; with/without mandrel | Not specified | Incremental cold forming; internal features possible with mandrel; high geometric flexibility [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/); wide size range [[61]](https://felss.com/en/maschine/rotary-swaging-machinesha/) | Not enumerated; high ratio is challenging (industrial user experience) [[62]](https://www.ro-ra.com/corporate-news/2023/swaging-of-tie-rod-tubes/) | Medium | Highly scalable for local reductions/steps/splines; good complement to tube/flow forming |
| Radial forging | Solid/tube + mandrel | Not specified | Produces inner diameters efficiently; complex hollow contours with mandrels; high-volume claim >10M shafts/year (broad) [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/) | Not enumerated in sources | Medium-high | Strong for high-volume robust shafts; good alternative to deep-hole drilling [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/) |
| Cross wedge rolling (CWR) | Cylindrical billet | Not specified | High productivity and material utilization [[75]](https://www.alekvs.com/cross-wedge-rolling-machine/); explicitly studied for hollow motor shafts [[76]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384053671_Design_and_simulation_of_cross_wedge_rolling_process_for_hollow_motor_shaft) | Central cracking (internal cavities) [[78]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/363857190_Investigation_and_prediction_of_central_cracking_in_cross_wedge_rolling); ovality/necking monitored [[79]](https://www.researchsquare.com/article/rs-3819506/latest.pdf) | High: preform still needs finish operations | Excellent for near-net preforms in very high volume, with strong process control |
| Backward/forward extrusion (mandrel) | Billet | Not specified | Standard bulk forming option in press makers’ portfolios [[84]](https://www.schulergroup.com/major/us/technologien/produkte/grundlagen_massivumformung_fliesspressen/index.html) | Mandrel friction generates heat in hollow extrusion [[85]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4701/8/6/380) | Medium-high | High-volume potential; tool/thermal management critical |
| Tube hydroforming | Tube | Aluminum and AHSS in Schuler lines [[87]](https://hydroforming.schulergroup.com/en/parts/index.html) | Complex shapes; integrated bending/preforming/hydroforming/laser cutting possible [[87]](https://hydroforming.schulergroup.com/en/parts/index.html) | Buckling, wrinkling, bursting [[90]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222018107_Hydroforming_of_aluminum_extrusion_tubes_for_automotive_applications_Part_I_Buckling_wrinkling_and_bursting_analyses_of_aluminum_tubes)[[91]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/260814621_Wrinkling_Study_in_Tube_Hydroforming_Process) | Medium-high | More common for structural tubes; niche for shafts due to runout demands |
| Electromagnetic forming / MPF / EMPT | Tube/sleeve localized | Conductive metals; process description general [[93]](https://www.bmax.com/technology/forming) | High-speed deformation; reduced springback; one-sided die [[93]](https://www.bmax.com/technology/forming); industrial EMPT systems offered [[92]](https://www.pstproducts.com/en/empt_machine_systems_pstproducts.html) | Coil rigidity issue in industrial solenoid coil contexts [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1526612524001270) | Medium | Best as a localized calibration/joining tool rather than full shaft shaping |
| Powder metallurgy + joining/brazing | Powder compacts / PM parts | PM alloys; conventional PM process steps [[96]](https://www.gknpm.com/en/technologies/conventional-powder-metallurgy/) | Hollow sections can be created by combining/sintering/brazing PM parts [[95]](https://www.fine-sinter.com/en/products/pmp/technology/panel3/post-11.php) | Not enumerated | High (joining + finishing) | Good for complex subfeatures; less obvious for full high-speed shafts |

### 4.2 Baseline comparison vs “machining hollow from solid” (important for decision-making)
Even though your question focuses on forming, many plants still benchmark against **deep-hole drilling**.

Deep-hole drilling is equipment- and energy-intensive:
- Gun drilling can demand 300–2000 psi coolant and pumping power exceeding cutting power (example: 1 hp cutting vs 5 hp pumping) [[111]](https://todaysmachiningworld.com/magazine/how-it-works-drilling-deep/).  
- BTA/STS drilling is much faster (stated **5–6× faster** than gun drilling) but typically requires dedicated machines [[111]](https://todaysmachiningworld.com/magazine/how-it-works-drilling-deep/).  
- Industrial BTA machines for shafts list high spindle power (up to **113 kW**) and coolant flow (up to **1500 lpm**) with large coolant tanks (up to **10,000 L**) [[112]](https://www.precihole.com/machine-by-series/bta-deep-hole-drilling-machine).

**Implication:** If a forming route can create the hollow near-net (radial forging, extrusion, flow forming from tube), it can avoid a major CAPEX/OPEX and yield risk center—at the cost of more complex forming dies and possibly more stringent forming defect controls.

---

## 5) What manufacturing routes are most suitable for NEV EDU hollow motor shafts (2026 view)

Below are **recommended manufacturing routes** by shaft “type” and performance tier, grounded in the industrial adoption signals and capability claims in the captured sources.

### 5.1 Most suitable routes for mainstream high-volume steel hollow rotor shafts

#### Route 1 (tube-based, high-speed optimized): **Precision tube → flow forming → swaging/spin-form end features → heat treat → grind → balance**
**Why it fits**
- Explicitly used for rotor shafts with very tight runout: flow forming route with **radial run-out < 0.05 mm** and integrated cooling fins/tube capability [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/).  
- Hollow cavity is valuable for cooling in powerful motors [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318); tube-based forming naturally supports thin-wall hollows and internal features.  
- Downstream finishing (grinding/balance) aligns with acceptance frameworks (runout ISO 1101 concepts [[12]](https://www.schlenker-spannwerkzeuge.de/en/wiki/circular-runout/)[[13]](https://www.keyence.com/ss/products/measure-sys/gd-and-t/type/run-out-tolerance.jsp)[[14]](https://www.iso.org/obp/ui/), balancing ISO framework [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)[[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)).

**Typical steps**
1) Buy seamless/cold-drawn precision tube [[52]](https://www.mannesmann.com/en/tubes-pipes)[[53]](https://www.mannesmann-precision-tubes.com/en/index.html) (or tube made via piercing + finishing [[58]](https://www.sms-group.com/plants/seamless-tube-plants))  
2) Flow form to final OD/ID profile and thin-wall regions [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html)[[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/)  
3) Rotary swage/spin-form ends for stepped diameters, collars, assembly shoulders [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/)[[67]](https://grotnes.com/spinning/)  
4) Heat treat appropriate to design (induction harden is explicitly part of industrial process chains in joined-shaft example) [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)  
5) Hard machining / grinding (IT3-quality grinding capability explicitly referenced for hollow rotor shaft example) [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)  
6) Cleanliness wash/pack [[101]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/en/products/electric-motor/rotor-shaft/)  
7) Dynamic balance per ISO, ensuring key convention compliance if keyed [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)

**Risk controls**
- Tight control of tube quality (wall thickness eccentricity) and forming stability.
- Runout verification after heat treatment and after any joining/pressing operations (IEC acceptance environment ties vibration/runout) [[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[11]](https://www.normsplash.com/Samples/IEC/169391251/IEC-60034-14-2018-en-fr.pdf).

#### Route 2 (for thick-wall / robust shafts): **Radial forging with mandrel (or extrusion) → finish machining → heat treat → grind → balance**
**Why it fits**
- Radial forging is positioned as efficient for making inner diameters and complex hollow contours with mandrels, avoiding costly ID machining [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).  
- Fits very high volumes (broadly evidenced by “>10 million shafts per year” claim for radial forging output) [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/).  

**Typical steps**
1) Tube or solid preform  
2) Radial forge with mandrel to generate ID and stepped OD [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/)  
3) Machine datum features and spline/gear interfaces (supplier capability notes “all common spline or gear types” on rotor shafts) [[101]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/en/products/electric-motor/rotor-shaft/)  
4) Heat treat + distortion control (industrial chains include induction hardening; production equipment emphasizes low-distortion carburizing for drivetrain parts) [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)[[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html)  
5) Grind critical journals and rotor-fit diameters [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)[[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html)  
6) Balance [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)

**Risk controls**
- Process control for internal surface integrity and concentricity; NDT strategy may be warranted depending on defect risk (UT approaches for hollow shafts exist industrially, albeit in other industries) [[118]](https://www.nds.actemium.de/en/products/ultrasonic-inspection-of-hollow-axles/).

### 5.2 Most suitable routes where an “assembled hollow rotor shaft” architecture is preferred

Assembled hollow shafts are not theoretical—they are explicitly industrialized and scaling globally.

- thyssenkrupp describes rotor shafts as an **assembled design**, with the key advantage being a hollow shaft cavity usable for cooling; they also state planned production of **~1.5 million rotor shafts** in the fiscal year referenced in the 2023 release and note global expansion to China/Mexico [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318).  
- Hirschvogel provides an explicit joined-shaft manufacturing chain including **laser welding** and subsequent hard finishing [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).

#### Route 3 (assembled/joined): **Two (or more) formed halves → machining → laser welding → induction harden → hard machine/grind → balance**
**Why it fits**
- Enables extremely thin walls and complex functional integration while keeping forming complexity manageable per half [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).  
- Laser welding is highlighted as enabling high-strength reproducible joints in high volume for powertrain components including rotor shafts [[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/).  
- Matches thyssenkrupp’s scaling story for assembled rotor shafts [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318).

**Typical chain (source-explicit exemplar)**
- “forged, machined, laser-welded, induction hardened, hard machined and ground” [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).

**Key engineering cautions**
- Weld distortion management is critical: distortion directly impacts runout and therefore vibration acceptance [[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[11]](https://www.normsplash.com/Samples/IEC/169391251/IEC-60034-14-2018-en-fr.pdf). Laser processes are positioned as reproducible and supportive of compact designs [[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/), which helps, but process control remains central.

### 5.3 Where CWR is most suitable
#### Route 4 (preform in extreme volume): **CWR hollow preform → calibration/forging → machining → HT → grind**
**Why it fits**
- CWR is explicitly studied for hollow motor shafts and is recognized for productivity and improved properties [[76]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384053671_Design_and_simulation_of_cross_wedge_rolling_process_for_hollow_motor_shaft)[[75]](https://www.alekvs.com/cross-wedge-rolling-machine/).  
- Best where production volume is enormous and near-net preforms save material and machining.

**Cautions**
- Central cracking risk (internal cavities) must be prevented through billet quality, process window control, and inspection strategy [[78]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/363857190_Investigation_and_prediction_of_central_cracking_in_cross_wedge_rolling).

### 5.4 Which routes are generally *less suitable* as the primary method (but useful as auxiliaries)
- **Tube hydroforming**: strong for complex tubular structures, but defect modes (buckling/wrinkling/bursting) are prominent [[90]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222018107_Hydroforming_of_aluminum_extrusion_tubes_for_automotive_applications_Part_I_Buckling_wrinkling_and_bursting_analyses_of_aluminum_tubes)[[91]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/260814621_Wrinkling_Study_in_Tube_Hydroforming_Process), and keeping tight runout for a high-speed rotor shaft is typically harder than with flow forming/swaging. Best as niche/local shaping if at all.  
- **Electromagnetic forming / MPF / EMPT**: excellent for localized calibration or joining with reduced springback [[93]](https://www.bmax.com/technology/forming), but tooling challenges (coil rigidity) and the need for pulsed power systems make it more of a specialized step than the core shaft-making route [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1526612524001270)[[92]](https://www.pstproducts.com/en/empt_machine_systems_pstproducts.html).  
- **Powder metallurgy hollow-by-assembly/brazing**: viable for complex shapes and certain hollow subassemblies [[95]](https://www.fine-sinter.com/en/products/pmp/technology/panel3/post-11.php)[[96]](https://www.gknpm.com/en/technologies/conventional-powder-metallurgy/), but as a full EDU rotor shaft route it usually raises fatigue/defect concerns (not source-quantified here) and often increases joining/inspection burden.

---

## 6) Practical decision framework (how to choose in an NEV EDU program)

### 6.1 If the shaft is **high-speed, thin-wall, cooling-integrated**
Prioritize:
1) **Tube-based + flow forming** (best evidence: runout <0.05 mm and internal cooling fins/tube integration) [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/)  
2) Complement with **rotary swaging/spin-forming** for end geometry and interface formation [[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/)[[67]](https://grotnes.com/spinning/)  
3) Finish by grinding (IT3-class capability exists in industrial rotor shaft offering) [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)  
4) Balance per ISO, and apply key convention rules if keyed [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)

### 6.2 If the shaft is **thick-wall / torque-heavy / cost-driven in huge volume**
Prioritize:
1) **Radial forging with mandrel** (inner diameter efficiently formed; high-volume orientation) [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/)  
2) Or **CWR** for preforms when volumes justify tooling and defect control investment [[75]](https://www.alekvs.com/cross-wedge-rolling-machine/)[[76]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384053671_Design_and_simulation_of_cross_wedge_rolling_process_for_hollow_motor_shaft)  
3) Strong downstream: heat treat distortion control (industrial focus) [[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html), grinding consolidation for takt time [[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html), and thorough runout/balance QA [[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[11]](https://www.normsplash.com/Samples/IEC/169391251/IEC-60034-14-2018-en-fr.pdf)[[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)

### 6.3 If the platform architecture favors modular assembly (or extreme lightweighting)
Prioritize:
- **Assembled/joined hollow rotor shaft** with laser welding:
  - adoption and scaling evidence: thyssenkrupp [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318)
  - explicit process chain: Hirschvogel [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)
  - process enabler: laser welding reproducibility and high-strength joints [[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/)

---

## 7) Open problems and near-term trends (through 2026)

1) **Cooling-through-shaft architectures are becoming more important**, pushing designs toward hollow/assembled shafts and routes that can integrate internal features (flow forming with internal fins and internal cooling tube integration is explicitly marketed) [[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/), consistent with the statement that powerful motors can use the hollow cavity for cooling [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318).

2) **NVH sensitivity to manufacturing deviations** is structurally increasing in electrified drivetrains; eccentricities and manufacturing deviations can materially change excitation behavior [[31]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10010-025-00816-z). This rewards manufacturing routes with inherently high concentricity/runout control (tube-based flow forming) and punishes routes with distortion risk unless carefully managed (welded assemblies, aggressive heat treatment).

3) **Global scaling of rotor shaft production** is underway (thyssenkrupp expansion to China/Mexico; million-unit annual production scale) [[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318), which typically pushes the industry toward:
   - highly automated chipless forming steps,
   - reduced machining allowances,
   - consolidated finishing equipment (e.g., grinder consolidation themes) [[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html),
   - and stable joining processes (laser welding) [[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/)[[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf).

4) **Inspection of hollow rotating parts** will remain a differentiator. Industrial UT systems for hollow shafts show that fast, repeatable internal inspection is feasible (10 min/axis, 97% availability) [[118]](https://www.nds.actemium.de/en/products/ultrasonic-inspection-of-hollow-axles/), but adapting inspection philosophy to smaller, faster automotive shafts (and to welded assemblies) is a continuing engineering effort.

---

### Bottom line (most suitable manufacturing routes)
- **Best overall for many NEV EDU hollow rotor shafts (especially high-speed, cooling-ready):**  
  **Precision tube → flow forming → swaging/spin-forming → heat treatment → grinding → balancing** [[66]](https://leifeldms.com/en/index.html)[[114]](https://wf-machinery.com/end-products-overview/components-for-e-drive-systems/rotor-shafts/)[[60]](https://felss.com/en/technologies/rotary-swaging/)[[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)[[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)

- **Best overall for extremely high-volume, robust, torque-heavy shafts where near-net preforms matter:**  
  **Radial forging (mandrel) or CWR preform → machining → heat treat → grinding → balancing** [[81]](https://www.gfm.at/products/radial-forging-automotive/)[[75]](https://www.alekvs.com/cross-wedge-rolling-machine/)[[76]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384053671_Design_and_simulation_of_cross_wedge_rolling_process_for_hollow_motor_shaft)[[102]](https://jtekt-pavilion.jp/en/ev-pavilion/production-system/index.html)[[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)

- **Best when architecture strongly benefits from modularity and extreme lightweighting:**  
  **Joined hollow shaft (formed parts) → machining → laser welding → induction hardening → hard machining/grinding → balancing** [[99]](https://www.hirschvogel.com/fileadmin/editor/05_News_Media/Downloads/Media/Produktbroschuere_EN.pdf)[[25]](https://www.trumpf.com/en_US/solutions/industries/automotive/e-mobility/laser-welding-of-e-mobility-powertrain-e-drives/)[[98]](https://www.thyssenkrupp-automotive-technology.com/en/press-detail/thyssenkrupp-dynamic-components-celebrates-10-years-of-rotor-shafts-for-electric-motors-and-continues-its-global-expansion-strategy-229318)

If you want, I can also provide a “reference process specification checklist” (CTQs: runout datum scheme, balance grade strategy, fit tolerances, cleanliness gates, and NDT plan) mapped to the ISO/IEC/DIN frameworks captured here [[1]](https://www.iso.org/standard/27092.html)[[9]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/56564/a73f83aa7a434a91ae52dce208843fc0/ISO-21940-32-2012.pdf)[[10]](https://www.vde-verlag.de/iec-normen/preview-pdf/info_iec60034-14%7Bed4.0.RLV%7Den.pdf)[[19]](https://www.skf.com/group/products/rolling-bearings/principles-of-rolling-bearing-selection/bearing-selection-process/bearing-interfaces/tolerances-and-resultant-fits)[[23]](https://www.eassistant.eu/fileadmin/dokumente/eassistant/etc/HTMLHandbuch/en/eAssistantHandbch15.html)[[24]](https://www.mesys.ch/doc/DIN743_CalculationBasis.pdf).
